content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 12, 2009

Michele Beal Bagneris
Pasadena City Attorney
100 North Garfield Ave, Suite N210
Pasadena, CA 91109-7215

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-09-237

Dear Mr. Bagneris:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest
provision of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Fair Political Practices
Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) In addition, our advice is based
solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if
any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or
Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does Vice Mayor Victor Gordo have a conflict of interest under the Act regarding
decisions the Pasadena City Council will make given his wife's investments in the
CalSTRS investment program?

CONCLUSION

Vice Mayor Gordo does not have a conflict of interest under the Act based on his
interest in his wife's CalSTRS pension.

___________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-237
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the city of Pasadena. You are requesting advice on
behalf of Vice Mayor Victor M. Gordo as to whether he has a conflict of interest under
the Act.

The Pasadena City Council (the "City Council") will be making decisions
regarding developing property that the California State Teachers' Retirement System
("CalSTRS") owns. The city council will be deciding whether to approve a project to
build an office building in Pasadena's Playhouse District (the "Project"). Several
applications will be before the City Council, including: a Conditional Use Permit, two
Minor Conditional Use Permits, an Adjustment Permit, and an Environmental Impact
Report. The City's Planning and Design Review Commissions completed their reviews
and recommendations and submitted them to the City Council for approval. The
decisions will be before the City Council on November 16.

Vice Mayor Gordo is an elected member of the City Council and his wife is a
school teacher with the El Monte School District. In that capacity, she is enrolled in the
CalSTRS retirement program. Both she and her employer make contributions to the
program. His wife takes no income from the account, and will not for at least ten more
years. If this account qualifies as an "investment" under the Act, Vice Mayor Gordo's
community property interest is more than $2,000.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step One: Is Vice Mayor Gordo a public official for purposes of the Act?

As a member of the Pasadena City Council, Vice Mayor Gordo is a public official
under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in making,
or otherwise use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic interests.
(Regulations 18702.1-18702.4.)

Step Two: Will Vice Mayor Gordo be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision?

The City Council will be reviewing and making decisions about a building project
in Pasadena's theatre district. Consequently, Vice Mayor Gordo will be called upon to

File No. A-09-237
Page No. 3

make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence these
decisions. (Regulations 18702.1-18702.4.)

Step Three: What are Vice Mayor Gordo's economic interests?

A public official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests:

1. An economic interest in any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

2. An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)

3. An economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 18703(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual
owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or
greater. (Section 82030(a).)

4. An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

5. An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his
or her immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

In addition to his own economic interests, Vice Mayor Gordo has an interest, for
purposes of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, in his wife's investments valued at
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103.) You have described Vice Mayor Gordo's wife's
CalSTRS pension program, but no other economic interests. Consequently, we will
analyze only this interest, and you are welcome to write again should there be additional
facts.

Vice Mayor Gordo's wife works for the El Monte School District and contributes
to the CalSTRS pension plan. Consequently, Vice Mayor Gordo has two possible
economic interests: 1) an investment interest in this wife's retirement plan through
CalSTRS to the extent that his interest is $2,000 or more and 2) his community share in
the promised income when his wife begins drawing from her pension.

File No. A-09-237
Page No. 4

Retirement plans, to the extent they consist of investment in the stock of a
business entity ordinarily, be treated as an economic interest in that business
entity if the amount invested meets the statutory threshold indicated above. (Section
82034.) The Act, however, provides that the term "investment" does not include "interest
in a government defined-benefit pension plan." (Id.) Accordingly, Vice Mayor Gordo
does not have an economic interest in his wife's CalSTRS retirement account because it
is a government defined-benefit pension plan.

While Vice Mayor Gordo's wife is not yet drawing income from her pension,
"promised income" is considered income under the Act. (Regulation 18703.3(a).) The
Act, however, excludes from the definition of income any salary from a governmental
entity. Additionally, the Act excludes any payment received under a defined benefit
pension plan qualified under Internal Revenue Code section 401(a) from the definition of
"income." (Section 82030(b)(11).) Because Vice Mayor Gordo's income interest would
be from CalSTRS, a qualifying defined benefit pension program, he has no income
interest and therefore no promised income interest.

Because Vice Mayor Gordo does not have an economic interest in the upcoming
decisions, we will not engage in the remaining steps of the analysis.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

interest in his wife's CaISTRS pension.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Vice Mayor Victor Gordo have a conflict of interest under the Act regarding
decisions the Pasadena City Council will make given his wife's investments in the
CaISTRS investment program?

CONCLUSION: Vice Mayor Gordo does not have a conflict of interest under the Act based on his
interest in his wife's CaISTRS pension.
I
